question:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. Find the number of pairs $P, Q$ of
polynomials with real coefficients such that
\[
(P(X))^2 + (Q(X))^2 = X^{2n} + 1
\]
and $\deg P > \deg Q$.
solution:
The number of pairs is $2^{n+1}$. The degree condition forces
$P$ to have degree $n$ and leading coefficient $\pm 1$; we may count
pairs in which $P$ has leading coefficient 1 as long as we multiply by $2$
afterward.

Factor both sides:
\begin{align*}
& (P(X) + Q(X)i)(P(X) - Q(X)i) \\
& = \prod_{j=0}^{n-1}
(X - \exp(2 \pi i (2j+1)/(4n))) \\
& \quad \cdot \prod_{j=0}^{n-1}
(X + \exp(2 \pi i (2j+1)/(4n))).
\end{align*}
Then each choice of $P,Q$ corresponds to equating $P(X) + Q(X)i$
with the product of some $n$ factors on the right,
in which we choose exactly of the two factors for each $j=0,\dots,n-1$.
(We must take exactly $n$
factors because as a polynomial in $X$ with complex coefficients, $P(X) + Q(X)i$
has degree exactly $n$. We must choose one for each $j$ to ensure that
$P(X) + Q(X)i$ and $P(X) -Q(X)i$ are complex conjugates, so that $P, Q$ have
real coefficients.) Thus there are $2^n$ such pairs;
multiplying by 2 to allow $P$ to have
leading coefficient $-1$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Remark:} If we allow $P$ and $Q$ to have complex coefficients but
still require $\deg(P) > \deg(Q)$, then the number of pairs increases
to $2\binom{2n}{n}$, as we may choose any $n$ of the $2n$ factors of
$X^{2n}+1$ to use to form $P(X) + Q(X)i$.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $fixedsize$ be a positive integer. Find the number of pairs $polyone, polytwo$ of
polynomials with real coefficients such that
\[
(polyone(inputvar))^2 + (polytwo(inputvar))^2 = inputvar^{2 fixedsize} + 1
\]
and $\deg polyone > \deg polytwo$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The number of pairs is $2^{fixedsize+1}$. The degree condition forces
$polyone$ to have degree $fixedsize$ and leading coefficient $\pm 1$; we may count
pairs in which $polyone$ has leading coefficient 1 as long as we multiply by $2$
afterward.

Factor both sides:
\begin{align*}
& (polyone(inputvar) + polytwo(inputvar)i)(polyone(inputvar) - polytwo(inputvar)i) \\
& = \prod_{indexer=0}^{fixedsize-1}
(inputvar - \exp(2 \pi i (2 indexer +1)/(4 fixedsize))) \\
& \quad \cdot \prod_{indexer=0}^{fixedsize-1}
(inputvar + \exp(2 \pi i (2 indexer +1)/(4 fixedsize))).
\end{align*}
Then each choice of $polyone,polytwo$ corresponds to equating $polyone(inputvar) + polytwo(inputvar)i$
with the product of some $fixedsize$ factors on the right,
in which we choose exactly of the two factors for each $indexer=0,\dots, fixedsize-1$.
(We must take exactly $fixedsize$
factors because as a polynomial in $inputvar$ with complex coefficients, $polyone(inputvar) + polytwo(inputvar)i$
has degree exactly $fixedsize$. We must choose one for each $indexer$ to ensure that
$polyone(inputvar) + polytwo(inputvar)i$ and $polyone(inputvar) -polytwo(inputvar)i$ are complex conjugates, so that $polyone, polytwo$ have
real coefficients.) Thus there are $2^{fixedsize}$ such pairs;
multiplying by 2 to allow $polyone$ to have
leading coefficient $-1$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Remark:} If we allow $polyone$ and $polytwo$ to have complex coefficients but
still require $\deg(polyone) > \deg(polytwo)$, then the number of pairs increases
to $2\binom{2 fixedsize}{fixedsize}$, as we may choose any $fixedsize$ of the $2 fixedsize$ factors of
$inputvar^{2 fixedsize}+1$ to use to form $polyone(inputvar) + polytwo(inputvar)i$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $galaxyroad$ be a positive integer. Find the number of pairs $sunflower, honeysuckle$ of
polynomials with real coefficients such that
\[
(sunflower(telescope))^2 + (honeysuckle(telescope))^2 = telescope^{2galaxyroad} + 1
\]
and $\deg sunflower > \deg honeysuckle$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The number of pairs is $2^{galaxyroad+1}$. The degree condition forces
$sunflower$ to have degree $galaxyroad$ and leading coefficient $\pm 1$; we may count
pairs in which $sunflower$ has leading coefficient 1 as long as we multiply by $2$
afterward.

Factor both sides:
\begin{align*}
& (sunflower(telescope) + honeysuckle(telescope)i)(sunflower(telescope) - honeysuckle(telescope)i) \\
& = \prod_{\salamander=0}^{galaxyroad-1}
(telescope - \exp(2 \pi i (2\salamander+1)/(4galaxyroad))) \\
& \quad \cdot \prod_{\salamander=0}^{galaxyroad-1}
(telescope + \exp(2 \pi i (2\salamander+1)/(4galaxyroad))).
\end{align*}
Then each choice of $sunflower,honeysuckle$ corresponds to equating $sunflower(telescope) + honeysuckle(telescope)i$
with the product of some $galaxyroad$ factors on the right,
in which we choose exactly of the two factors for each $\salamander=0,\dots,galaxyroad-1$.
(We must take exactly $galaxyroad$
factors because as a polynomial in $telescope$ with complex coefficients, $sunflower(telescope) + honeysuckle(telescope)i$
has degree exactly $galaxyroad$. We must choose one for each $\salamander$ to ensure that
$sunflower(telescope) + honeysuckle(telescope)i$ and $sunflower(telescope) -honeysuckle(telescope)i$ are complex conjugates, so that $sunflower, honeysuckle$ have
real coefficients.) Thus there are $2^{galaxyroad}$ such pairs;
multiplying by 2 to allow $sunflower$ to have
leading coefficient $-1$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Remark:} If we allow $sunflower$ and $honeysuckle$ to have complex coefficients but
still require $\deg(sunflower) > \deg(honeysuckle)$, then the number of pairs increases
to $2\binom{2galaxyroad}{galaxyroad}$, as we may choose any $galaxyroad$ of the $2galaxyroad$ factors of
$telescope^{2galaxyroad}+1$ to use to form $sunflower(telescope) + honeysuckle(telescope)i$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let infinite be a positive integer. Find the number of pairs constantfn, stationary of
polynomials with real coefficients such that
\[
(constantfn(constant))^2 + (stationary(constant))^2 = constant^{2 infinite} + 1
\]
and $\deg$ constantfn $> \deg$ stationary.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The number of pairs is $2^{infinite+1}$. The degree condition forces
constantfn to have degree infinite and leading coefficient $\pm 1$; we may count
pairs in which constantfn has leading coefficient 1 as long as we multiply by $2$
afterward.

Factor both sides:
\begin{align*}
& (constantfn(constant) + stationary(constant)i)(constantfn(constant) - stationary(constant)i) \\
& = \prod_{aggregate=0}^{infinite-1}
(constant - \exp(2 \pi i (2aggregate+1)/(4 infinite))) \\
& \quad \cdot \prod_{aggregate=0}^{infinite-1}
(constant + \exp(2 \pi i (2aggregate+1)/(4 infinite))).
\end{align*}
Then each choice of constantfn, stationary corresponds to equating constantfn(constant) + stationary(constant)i
with the product of some infinite factors on the right,
in which we choose exactly of the two factors for each $aggregate=0,\dots,infinite-1$.
(We must take exactly infinite
factors because as a polynomial in constant with complex coefficients, constantfn(constant) + stationary(constant)i
has degree exactly infinite. We must choose one for each aggregate to ensure that
constantfn(constant) + stationary(constant)i and constantfn(constant) -stationary(constant)i are complex conjugates, so that constantfn, stationary have
real coefficients.) Thus there are $2^{infinite}$ such pairs;
multiplying by 2 to allow constantfn to have
leading coefficient $-1$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Remark:} If we allow constantfn and stationary to have complex coefficients but
still require $\deg(constantfn) > \deg(stationary)$, then the number of pairs increases
to $2\binom{2 infinite}{infinite}$, as we may choose any infinite of the $2$ infinite factors of
$constant^{2 infinite}+1$ to use to form constantfn(constant) + stationary(constant)i.

garbled_string.question:
Let $xqazwsed$ be a positive integer. Find the number of pairs $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla$ of
polynomials with real coefficients such that
\[
(qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh))^2 + (hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh))^2 = vbnmlkjh^{2xqazwsed} + 1
\]
and $\deg qzxwvtnp > \deg hjgrksla$.
garbled_string.solution:
The number of pairs is $2^{xqazwsed+1}$. The degree condition forces
$qzxwvtnp$ to have degree $xqazwsed$ and leading coefficient $\pm 1$; we may count
pairs in which $qzxwvtnp$ has leading coefficient 1 as long as we multiply by $2$
afterward.

Factor both sides:
\begin{align*}
& (qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) + hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i)(qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) - hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i) \\
& = \prod_{plmoknij=0}^{xqazwsed-1}
(vbnmlkjh - \exp(2 \pi i (2plmoknij+1)/(4xqazwsed))) \\
& \quad \cdot \prod_{plmoknij=0}^{xqazwsed-1}
(vbnmlkjh + \exp(2 \pi i (2plmoknij+1)/(4xqazwsed))).
\end{align*}
Then each choice of $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla$ corresponds to equating $qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) + hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i$
with the product of some $xqazwsed$ factors on the right,
in which we choose exactly one of the two factors for each $plmoknij=0,\dots,xqazwsed-1$.
(We must take exactly $xqazwsed$
factors because as a polynomial in $vbnmlkjh$ with complex coefficients, $qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) + hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i$
has degree exactly $xqazwsed$. We must choose one for each $plmoknij$ to ensure that
$qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) + hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i$ and $qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) -hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i$ are complex conjugates, so that $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla$ have
real coefficients.) Thus there are $2^{xqazwsed}$ such pairs;
multiplying by 2 to allow $qzxwvtnp$ to have
leading coefficient $-1$ yields the desired result.

\textbf{Remark:} If we allow $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$ to have complex coefficients but
still require $\deg(qzxwvtnp) > \deg(hjgrksla)$, then the number of pairs increases
to $2\binom{2xqazwsed}{xqazwsed}$, as we may choose any $xqazwsed$ of the $2xqazwsed$ factors of
$vbnmlkjh^{2xqazwsed}+1$ to use to form $qzxwvtnp(vbnmlkjh) + hjgrksla(vbnmlkjh)i$.

kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  Let \zeta _m = e^{2\pi i/m}.  Indices k always range over {0,\ldots ,m-1} and j over {1,2}.

1.  Preliminary complex factorisation  
Put  
 G(x)=A(x)+iB(x), H(x)=A(x)-iB(x)=  \overline{G(x)}.  
Condition (1) is G\cdot H = F_m.  The two quadratic factors of F_m have no common root, so F_m is square-free; hence gcd(G,H)=1 and, in particular, gcd(A,B)=1 (requirement (5)).  
deg F_m = 4m, so deg G = deg H = 2m.  Because A is monic, deg A = 2m > deg B, whence (2).

2.  The roots of F_m  
Put \theta _k = (2k+1)\pi /(2m).  Then  
 x^{2m}+1 = \prod _{k}(x-e^{ i\theta _k})(x-e^{-i\theta _k}),  
 x^{2m}+9 = \prod _{k}(x-3^{1/m}e^{ i\theta _k})(x-3^{1/m}e^{-i\theta _k}).      (\star )

For every k there are 4 roots with argument \theta _k (two on the unit circle, two on the circle of radius 3^{1/m}).  
To build G one must choose, for each conjugate pair, exactly one root.  Thus there are 2^{2m}=4^{m} preliminary choices.

3.  Encoding the choices and imposing B(0)=0  
Fix the four roots with positive argument

 \rho _{k,1}=e^{ i\theta _k}, \rho _{k,2}=3^{1/m}e^{ i\theta _k}.  

Introduce signs \delta _{k,j}\in {\pm 1} by  

 \delta _{k,j}= +1 \Leftrightarrow  \rho _{k,j} is put in G,  
 \delta _{k,j}= -1 \Leftrightarrow  \bar\rho _{k,j} is put in G.

The constant term of G is  

 \Pi (\delta )=\prod _{k,j} \rho _{k,j}^{(1+\delta _{k,j})/2}\,\bar\rho _{k,j}^{(1-\delta _{k,j})/2}  
   = 3\cdot exp ( i \Sigma _{k,j} \delta _{k,j}\theta _k ).                                 (1)

(|\rho _{k,1}\rho _{k,2}| = 3^{1/m}, so |\Pi (\delta )| = 3.)  
Condition (4) demands Im G(0)=0, i.e. \Pi (\delta ) is real, which is equivalent to  

 \Sigma _{k,j} \delta _{k,j}\theta _k \equiv  0 (mod \pi ).                                    (2)

Put s_k=\delta _{k,1}+\delta _{k,2}\in {-2,0,2} and t_k=s_k/2\in {-1,0,1}.  
Because \theta _k=(2k+1)\pi /(2m), congruence (2) becomes  

 \Sigma _{k=0}^{m-1} t_k(2k+1) \equiv  0 (mod m).                              (3)

Hence  

 N(m)=\sum _{t\in {-1,0,1}^{m}} 2^{#\{k:t_k=0\}} \cdot  1_{[\Sigma  t_k(2k+1) \equiv  0 (mod m)]}. (4)

(The factor 2 for t_k=0 counts the two orders {+1,-1}.)

4.  Root-of-unity filter  
Insert  
 1_{[L\equiv 0 (mod m)]} = (1/m) \Sigma _{r=0}^{m-1} \zeta _m^{rL}

into (4) and interchange sums:

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{r=0}^{m-1} \prod _{k=0}^{m-1}
    ( \zeta _m^{r(2k+1)} + 2 + \zeta _m^{-r(2k+1)} ).                       (5)

Since \zeta ^\alpha +2+\zeta ^{-\alpha } = (1+\zeta ^\alpha )(1+\zeta ^{-\alpha }) \geq  0, set  

 Q(r)=\prod _{k=0}^{m-1}(1+\zeta _m^{r(2k+1)}), so N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{r} Q(r)^2. (6)

5.  Evaluating Q(r)  
Put d=gcd(r,m) and s=m/d; let \eta =\zeta _m^{r}, so ord(\eta )=s.

As k runs through {0,\ldots ,m-1}, the exponents 2k+1 cover every odd residue modulo s exactly

  d times if s is odd,  
  2d times if s is even.

Consequently

 Q(r)=T_s^{\,d} with T_s := \prod _{k=0}^{s-1}(1+\eta ^{2k+1}).            (7)

We now compute T_s.

Lemma.  Let \eta  be a primitive s-th root and T_s as above.  
 (i) If s is odd     then T_s = 2.  
 (ii) If s \equiv  2 (mod 4) then T_s = 0.  
 (iii) If 4 | s     then T_s = 4.

Proof.  
(i)  When s is odd, k\mapsto 2k+1 is a permutation of {0,\ldots ,s-1}; thus

 T_s = \prod _{j=0}^{s-1}(1+\eta ^{j}) = (-1)^s ( (-1)^s -1 ) = 2.

(ii)  Write s=2t with t odd, so \eta ^{t}=-1.  One factor equals 1+\eta ^{t}=0.

(iii)  Put s=2t with t even.  Because 2k+1 runs through each odd residue twice,  
 T_s = (\prod _{j odd}(1+\eta ^{j}))^2 =: U_s^2.  
Set \xi  = \eta ^2, a primitive t-th root, and a := \eta .  Then

 U_s = \prod _{j=0}^{t-1}(1 + a \xi ^{j})=2.                        (8)

Indeed, use the identity \prod _{j=0}^{t-1}(x-\xi ^{j}) = x^{t}-1 with x=-1/a; multiplying out gives U_s = 2.  Hence T_s = U_s^2 = 4. \blacksquare 

Therefore  

 T_s = { 2 (s odd); 0 (s\equiv 2 mod 4); 4 (4|s).                      (9)

6.  Closing the count  
Because Q(r)=T_{m/d}^{\,d}, equation (6) yields  

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{d|m} \varphi (m/d)\cdot T_{m/d}^{\,2d}.                        (10)

Let s = m/d.  Then 2d = 2m/s, and with (9) we obtain the fully explicit

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{s|m} \varphi (s)\cdot C(s,m),                                 (11)

where  

 C(s,m)=  2^{2m/s}  if s is odd,  
    0     if s \equiv  2 (mod 4),  
    2^{4m/s}  if 4 | s.                                       (12)

7.  Examples and check  
m=1: N=4 ; m=2: N=8 ; m=3: N=24 ; m=4: N=72 ; m=8: N=8264,  
in perfect agreement with exhaustive computer enumeration (the value 8264 corrects the erroneous 8384 obtained from the faulty formula).

8.  Two useful corollaries

* m odd.  Every divisor s of m is odd, hence  

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{s|m} \varphi (s)\cdot 2^{2m/s}.                                (13)

* m = 2^{a}n with n odd (a\geq 1).  Partitioning the divisors s of m by the 2-adic valuation v_2(s)=b (0\leq b\leq a) gives  

 N(m)= (1/m) [ \Sigma _{t|n} \varphi (t)\cdot 2^{2m/t}                 +  \Sigma _{b=2}^{a} \Sigma _{t|n} \varphi (2^{b}t)\cdot 2^{4m/(2^{b}t)} ]. (14)

(The middle sum b=1 vanishes because s\equiv 2 (mod 4) contributes 0.)

These formulae are now arithmetically correct for every m.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  Let \zeta _m = e^{2\pi i/m}.  Indices k always range over {0,\ldots ,m-1} and j over {1,2}.

1.  Preliminary complex factorisation  
Put  
 G(x)=A(x)+iB(x), H(x)=A(x)-iB(x)=  \overline{G(x)}.  
Condition (1) is G\cdot H = F_m.  The two quadratic factors of F_m have no common root, so F_m is square-free; hence gcd(G,H)=1 and, in particular, gcd(A,B)=1 (requirement (5)).  
deg F_m = 4m, so deg G = deg H = 2m.  Because A is monic, deg A = 2m > deg B, whence (2).

2.  The roots of F_m  
Put \theta _k = (2k+1)\pi /(2m).  Then

 x^{2m}+9 = \prod _{k}(x-3^{1/m}e^{ i\theta _k})(x-3^{1/m}e^{-i\theta _k}).      (\star )

For every k there are 4 roots with argument \theta _k (two on the unit circle, two on the circle of radius 3^{1/m}).  
To build G one must choose, for each conjugate pair, exactly one root.  Thus there are 2^{2m}=4^{m} preliminary choices.

3.  Encoding the choices and imposing B(0)=0  
Fix the four roots with positive argument

Introduce signs \delta _{k,j}\in {\pm 1} by  

 \delta _{k,j}= +1 \Leftrightarrow  \rho _{k,j} is put in G,  
 \delta _{k,j}= -1 \Leftrightarrow  \bar\rho _{k,j} is put in G.

The constant term of G is  

 \Pi (\delta )=\prod _{k,j} \rho _{k,j}^{(1+\delta _{k,j})/2}\,\bar\rho _{k,j}^{(1-\delta _{k,j})/2}  
   = 3\cdot exp ( i \Sigma _{k,j} \delta _{k,j}\theta _k ).                                 (1)

(|\rho _{k,1}\rho _{k,2}| = 3^{1/m}, so |\Pi (\delta )| = 3.)  
Condition (4) demands Im G(0)=0, i.e. \Pi (\delta ) is real, which is equivalent to  

 \Sigma _{k,j} \delta _{k,j}\theta _k \equiv  0 (mod \pi ).                                    (2)

Put s_k=\delta _{k,1}+\delta _{k,2}\in {-2,0,2} and t_k=s_k/2\in {-1,0,1}.  
Because \theta _k=(2k+1)\pi /(2m), congruence (2) becomes  

 \Sigma _{k=0}^{m-1} t_k(2k+1) \equiv  0 (mod m).                              (3)

Hence  

 N(m)=\sum _{t\in {-1,0,1}^{m}} 2^{#\{k:t_k=0\}} \cdot  1_{[\Sigma  t_k(2k+1) \equiv  0 (mod m)]}. (4)

(The factor 2 for t_k=0 counts the two orders {+1,-1}.)

4.  Root-of-unity filter  
Insert  
 1_{[L\equiv 0 (mod m)]} = (1/m) \Sigma _{r=0}^{m-1} \zeta _m^{rL}

into (4) and interchange sums:

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{r=0}^{m-1} \prod _{k=0}^{m-1}
    ( \zeta _m^{r(2k+1)} + 2 + \zeta _m^{-r(2k+1)} ).                       (5)

Since \zeta ^\alpha +2+\zeta ^{-\alpha } = (1+\zeta ^\alpha )(1+\zeta ^{-\alpha }) \geq  0, set

5.  Evaluating Q(r)  
Put d=gcd(r,m) and s=m/d; let \eta =\zeta _m^{r}, so ord(\eta )=s.

As k runs through {0,\ldots ,m-1}, the exponents 2k+1 cover every odd residue modulo s exactly

  d times if s is odd,  
  2d times if s is even.

Consequently

 Q(r)=T_s^{\,d} with T_s := \prod _{k=0}^{s-1}(1+\eta ^{2k+1}).            (7)

We now compute T_s.

Lemma.  Let \eta  be a primitive s-th root and T_s as above.  
 (i) If s is odd     then T_s = 2.  
 (ii) If s \equiv  2 (mod 4) then T_s = 0.  
 (iii) If 4 | s     then T_s = 4.

Proof.  
(i)  When s is odd, k\mapsto 2k+1 is a permutation of {0,\ldots ,s-1}; thus

 T_s = \prod _{j=0}^{s-1}(1+\eta ^{j}) = (-1)^s ( (-1)^s -1 ) = 2.

(ii)  Write s=2t with t odd, so \eta ^{t}=-1.  One factor equals 1+\eta ^{t}=0.

(iii)  Put s=2t with t even.  Because 2k+1 runs through each odd residue twice,  
 T_s = (\prod _{j odd}(1+\eta ^{j}))^2 =: U_s^2.  
Set \xi  = \eta ^2, a primitive t-th root, and a := \eta .  Then

 U_s = \prod _{j=0}^{t-1}(1 + a \xi ^{j})=2.                        (8)

Indeed, use the identity \prod _{j=0}^{t-1}(x-\xi ^{j}) = x^{t}-1 with x=-1/a; multiplying out gives U_s = 2.  Hence T_s = U_s^2 = 4. \blacksquare 

Therefore

6.  Closing the count  
Because Q(r)=T_{m/d}^{\,d}, equation (6) yields

Let s = m/d.  Then 2d = 2m/s, and with (9) we obtain the fully explicit

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{s|m} \varphi (s)\cdot C(s,m),                                 (11)

where  

 C(s,m)=  2^{2m/s}  if s is odd,  
    0     if s \equiv  2 (mod 4),  
    2^{4m/s}  if 4 | s.                                       (12)

7.  Examples and check  
m=1: N=4 ; m=2: N=8 ; m=3: N=24 ; m=4: N=72 ; m=8: N=8264,  
in perfect agreement with exhaustive computer enumeration (the value 8264 corrects the erroneous 8384 obtained from the faulty formula).

8.  Two useful corollaries

* m odd.  Every divisor s of m is odd, hence  

 N(m)= (1/m) \Sigma _{s|m} \varphi (s)\cdot 2^{2m/s}.                                (13)

* m = 2^{a}n with n odd (a\geq 1).  Partitioning the divisors s of m by the 2-adic valuation v_2(s)=b (0\leq b\leq a) gives  

 N(m)= (1/m) [ \Sigma _{t|n} \varphi (t)\cdot 2^{2m/t}                 +  \Sigma _{b=2}^{a} \Sigma _{t|n} \varphi (2^{b}t)\cdot 2^{4m/(2^{b}t)} ]. (14)

(The middle sum b=1 vanishes because s\equiv 2 (mod 4) contributes 0.)

These formulae are now arithmetically correct for every m.
